name: Tonatiuh
description: Tonatiuh, in Aztec mythology, is the sun god and the leader of Tollan, the heavens. He is depicted as a fierce warrior constantly battling the forces of darkness to bring light and warmth to the world. Tonatiuh demands constant nourishment in the form of human blood, and the Aztecs believed that human sacrifice was necessary to sustain him and prevent the world from plunging into eternal darkness. He is associated with the eagle, symbolizing his power and connection to the sky. His face is often depicted with a fiery red hue, representing the intense heat of the sun. According to the Aztec creation myth, Tonatiuh was born from the sacrificial fire of the gods. He is the fifth sun, the present era, and his reign will end in an earthquake. His journey across the sky each day is a perilous one, and he is accompanied by the souls of warriors who have died in battle. Without the sacrifices made to him, Tonatiuh would weaken and eventually fail, leading to the destruction of the world. He represents strength, courage, and the vital life force that sustains all living things. He is often shown with the glyph for 'Ollin', meaning movement or earthquake, foreshadowing his eventual demise.
subtitle: The Aztec Sun God and Warrior

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: The gods sacrificed themselves to create the fifth sun, Tonatiuh. He required constant sustenance in the form of human blood to keep moving.
keyMyths.source: Histoy of the Mexicans as Told By Their Paintings

keyMyths.title: Tonatiuh's Daily Journey
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh travels across the sky each day, battling the forces of darkness. He is accompanied by the souls of fallen warriors.

keyMyths.title: The End of the Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: The current era, ruled by Tonatiuh, is destined to end in a devastating earthquake. This event will bring about the next cycle of creation.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de

sources.description: Comprehensive account of Aztec culture and religion.
sources.text: Comprehensive account of Aztec culture and religion.

sources.text: A general overview of Aztec history and culture.

sources.author: Miller, Mary

sources.text: Analysis of Mesoamerican art, including depictions of deities.

worshipPractices.name: Human Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: The Aztecs believed that Tonatiuh required constant nourishment in the form of human blood to maintain his strength and continue his daily journey across the sky. Sacrifice was seen as a sacred duty to repay the debt owed to the sun for life and warmth.

worshipPractices.name: Ritualistic Warfare
worshipPractices.description: The Aztecs engaged in ritualistic warfare, known as flower wars, to capture prisoners for sacrifice to Tonatiuh and other deities. These battles were carefully orchestrated and served a religious purpose beyond territorial expansion.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: In addition to human sacrifice, the Aztecs offered other valuable items to Tonatiuh, such as precious stones, incense, and food. These offerings were intended to appease the god and ensure his continued benevolence.

festivals.name: Panquetzaliztli
festivals.description: A major festival dedicated to Huitzilopochtli, the god of war and the sun. It involved elaborate ceremonies, dances, and sacrifices to honor the sun's power and ensure victory in battle.

festivals.name: Toxcatl
festivals.timing: Fifth Month of the Aztec Calendar
festivals.description: A festival dedicated to Tezcatlipoca, a powerful god often associated with the sun and the night sky. It involved the sacrifice of a young man who had been impersonating the god for a year, symbolizing the cyclical nature of life and death.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tonatiuh's image remains a powerful symbol of Mexican identity, often depicted in art, murals, and popular culture. His association with the sun and sacrifice continues to evoke discussions about Aztec religion and its impact on Mexican history.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Tonatiuh are deeply intertwined with the Aztec creation myth, specifically the legend of the Fifth Sun. According to this narrative, the gods gathered at Teotihuacan to decide which of them would sacrifice themselves to become the new sun. Nanahuatzin, a humble and diseased god, bravely volunteered and cast himself into the sacred fire. However, the vain and wealthy god Tecuciztecatl hesitated, eventually following Nanahuatzin into the flames. Nanahuatzin emerged as Tonatiuh, the radiant sun, while Tecuciztecatl became the moon. Because Tecuciztecatl hesitated, the gods punished him by dimming his light. However, Tonatiuh, though born from sacrifice, was initially immobile. To grant him the strength to move across the sky, the other gods also sacrificed themselves, initiating the era of the Fifth Sun and establishing the need for continued sacrifice to sustain the sun's journey.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the central myths involving Tonatiuh revolves around his daily battle against the forces of darkness. Each day, he rises in the east and travels across the sky, providing light and warmth to the world. However, his journey is fraught with danger, as he must constantly fight against the stars and other celestial beings who seek to extinguish his light. At sunset, Tonatiuh descends into the underworld, where he continues his battle against the forces of darkness throughout the night. He emerges victorious each morning, renewing the cycle of life and reaffirming the importance of sacrifice. Another important myth describes the origin of the Fifth Sun and the need for human sacrifice to sustain Tonatiuh's energy. The Aztecs believed that without constant nourishment, Tonatiuh would weaken, and the world would plunge into eternal darkness.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of Tonatiuh was central to Aztec religious life, permeating nearly every aspect of their culture. Human sacrifice was the most prominent and controversial aspect of his cult, with victims offered to appease the sun god and ensure the continuation of the world. The Aztecs believed that the blood of sacrificial victims contained a vital life force that could replenish Tonatiuh's energy. Beyond human sacrifice, offerings of precious stones, incense, and food were also made to Tonatiuh. Temples dedicated to Tonatiuh were strategically located throughout the Aztec empire, serving as centers of worship and ritual. Priests played a crucial role in performing ceremonies and interpreting the will of the god. The solar calendar, with its complex system of cycles and dates, was also closely linked to the worship of Tonatiuh, dictating the timing of festivals and rituals.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tonatiuh's legacy extends far beyond the confines of ancient Aztec civilization. His image, often depicted as a fierce warrior with a solar disc on his chest, has become an enduring symbol of Mexican identity. Tonatiuh appears on the Mexican Coat of Arms, specifically in the center of the Stone of the Fifth Sun (commonly known as the Aztec Sun Stone), representing the origin of the world and the cyclical nature of time. His influence can be seen in contemporary art, literature, and popular culture, where he is often portrayed as a powerful and enigmatic figure. The debates surrounding the practice of human sacrifice in Aztec religion continue to fuel scholarly discussions about the complexities of ancient cultures and the challenges of interpreting historical evidence. Tonatiuh remains a potent reminder of the rich and complex heritage of the Aztec civilization.

shortDescription: Tonatiuh is the Aztec sun god, leader of Tollan. He requires nourishment of human blood to sustain the world and prevent eternal darkness.
